title: Death of Socrates
summary: In the early summer of 399 BCE the seventy-year-old Athenian philosopher Socrates, condemned by a jury of five hundred and one citizens on charges of impiety and corrupting the young, drank a cup of hemlock in the state prison of Athens and died among his friends. The trial, its verdict and its execution were legal in every particular, and yet the death of Socrates became, through the writings of his student Plato, one of the founding scenes of western philosophy and a permanent question mark over the relation between free thought and democratic politics.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Socrates was born in Athens around 470 BCE, the son of a stonemason called Sophroniscus and a midwife called Phaenarete. He served as a hoplite in the Peloponnesian War at Potidaea, Delium and Amphipolis, and in Plato's Symposium his physical endurance and personal bravery are recalled with warmth by younger comrades including the general Alcibiades. He held no political office beyond a single term as prytanis in 406 BCE, when he refused, alone among the presiding council, to put an illegal collective vote against the generals of Arginusae. Under the oligarchic regime of the Thirty Tyrants in 404 to 403 BCE he likewise refused an order to arrest Leon of Salamis for judicial murder, at genuine personal risk.

He wrote nothing. What we know of him comes from three near-contemporary sources: the philosophical dialogues of his pupil Plato, the more homely Memorabilia of Xenophon, and the comic parody of the Clouds, staged by Aristophanes in 423 BCE, in which a figure called Socrates presides over a Thinkery devoted to natural science and clever rhetoric. Even in Aristophanes it is clear that by his mid-forties Socrates was a well-known Athenian eccentric: barefoot, snub-nosed, given to buttonholing the confident on the topics they claimed to know most about, and to demonstrating, by patient question and answer, that they in fact knew rather less.

Around him gathered a loose circle of young aristocrats: Plato, Xenophon, Alcibiades, Critias, Charmides and many others. Two of them, Critias and Charmides, went on to prominent roles in the brutally oligarchic government of the Thirty. Alcibiades had earlier defected first to Sparta and then to Persia, contributing materially to Athens's defeat in the Peloponnesian War. When Athens surrendered to Sparta in 404 BCE and the democracy was restored a year later, the memory of these associations counted heavily against Socrates in the popular mind, even though he had personally refused to co-operate with the tyranny.

The restored democracy of 403 BCE had promised, in an amnesty, not to prosecute past political offences. But philosophical and religious accusations remained open. In the spring of 399 BCE three prosecutors laid a formal charge against Socrates at the office of the archon basileus: the young tragedian Meletus, the influential democratic politician Anytus, and a minor orator called Lycon. The indictment, preserved in Diogenes Laertius, ran: Socrates is guilty of refusing to recognise the gods recognised by the state, and of introducing new divinities, and of corrupting the youth. The proposed penalty was death.

image.caption: Marble bust of Socrates, Roman copy after a Greek original by Lysippus. Louvre, Paris.
image.credit: Photograph by Eric Gaba (Sting), via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The trial was held in the People's Court, the Heliaea, before a jury of five hundred and one adult male citizens drawn by lot from all ten tribes. Athenian trials were completed in a single day; each side was allotted a fixed water-clock time to speak, and the jury voted twice, first on guilt and, if a conviction resulted, on the sentence, choosing between the prosecutor's proposal and the defendant's counter-proposal.

Socrates conducted his own defence, in the direct, colloquial style he used in the market. Plato's Apology, written some years after the event but drawing on eyewitness memory, presents that defence as an insistence that his philosophical mission was a service to Athens itself, ordered by the Delphic oracle when it had told his friend Chaerephon that no man was wiser than Socrates. His practice of testing the wisdom of others, he said, had been in obedience to the god. He refused to weep, to bring in his three young sons, or to beg. He insisted that if acquitted on condition of giving up philosophy he would refuse, since he must obey the god rather than the court. The jury convicted, by roughly 280 votes to 221 in the calculation preserved by later sources.

Under Athenian law the convicted man was then required to propose an alternative penalty. Socrates suggested, in a passage that Plato records with obvious relish, that his real desert was to be maintained at public expense at the Prytaneum, the honour normally reserved for Olympic victors and public benefactors. On his friends' urging he then offered a fine of thirty minae, which Plato, Crito, Critobulus and Apollodorus among them offered to guarantee. The jury voted for the prosecution's proposal, death, and by a wider margin than the conviction itself.

Execution should ordinarily have followed at once, by administration of a preparation of poison hemlock (Conium maculatum). It was delayed by a technicality: the annual sacred embassy to the shrine of Apollo on Delos had been consecrated the day before the trial, and no execution was permitted until the ship returned. Contrary winds held it up for thirty days, which Socrates spent in prison receiving his friends. Plato's Crito, set on one of these mornings, has Crito urging him to escape into exile, an option quietly available to a man with wealthy friends and jailers susceptible to bribery. Socrates refuses on the ground that a citizen who has enjoyed the benefits of Athenian law cannot, when it turns against him, break it without contradiction.

The Phaedo, Plato's dialogue on the immortality of the soul, is set on the last day itself. Phaedo of Elis narrates the scene to Echecrates of Phlius, listing the disciples present: Apollodorus, Crito and his son Critobulus, Hermogenes, Epigenes, Aeschines, Antisthenes, Ctesippus, Menexenus, Simmias and Cebes of Thebes, Euclides and Terpsion of Megara, Phaedondes and others; Plato himself, we are told, was ill and absent. Socrates spent the day discussing whether the soul survives the body, bathed himself so as to spare the women the trouble of washing his corpse, and at sunset drank the cup the executioner brought him. As the coldness moved up his legs, he uncovered his face and said to Crito, We owe a cock to Asclepius; pay the debt and do not forget. The words, a routine thank-offering to the god of healing, are the last recorded of the western philosophical tradition's first martyr.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
In legal terms nothing happened. The verdict stood, no reversal was ever passed, and the amnesty of 403 BCE covered anyone who might have wished to prosecute Anytus, Meletus and Lycon in return. A later Athenian tradition, preserved in Diogenes Laertius, claimed that the city eventually repented, exiled Meletus, and executed Anytus; the story is almost certainly a moralising invention. In practice Anytus continued a prosperous political career.

The philosophical consequences were of a different order. Plato withdrew from public life, travelled in Megara and Egypt and Sicily, and in about 387 BCE founded the Academy in the grove of the hero Akademos on the outskirts of Athens, the first sustained institution of higher philosophical education in the Greek world. Almost all his surviving dialogues place Socrates at the centre. Xenophon, exiled from Athens shortly after the trial for his service to Cyrus the Younger and to Sparta, wrote his own Memorabilia and Apology of Socrates in defence of the man he had known. Antisthenes, another pupil, was claimed by later Cynics as their founder; Aristippus of Cyrene took Socratic ideas in a different direction, toward hedonism; Euclid of Megara developed the Megarian school; from Plato's Academy would emerge in the next generation the young Macedonian student Aristotle. The whole subsequent tradition of Greek philosophy, and through it much of western intellectual history, took as its starting point a man who had, in Cicero's famous phrase, called philosophy down from the heavens to the cities and houses of men.

Within Athens itself the trial became one of the standard exhibits in a longer argument about the character of the restored democracy. Isocrates, in his 353 BCE speech Antidosis, defended the philosophical life against the same suspicions that had been raised against Socrates. In the third century BCE the Stoics of the Painted Stoa, only a few hundred metres from where Socrates had once questioned the market crowds, made his self-possession before death a model of the sage's indifference to fortune. Roman Stoics such as Seneca and Epictetus would in turn hold up his composure in the last hours as the archetype of the philosophical death, a model they consciously imitated in their own.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Socrates founded no school, wrote no book, and codified no doctrine, and yet almost every subsequent western school of philosophy has claimed him. Plato, Aristotle, the Cynics, the Stoics, the Sceptics, the Neoplatonists, the Renaissance humanists, Montaigne, the eighteenth-century philosophes, Kierkegaard, Nietzsche and Foucault all read themselves through him. His characteristic technique, the elenchus, of drawing out contradictions in a partner's confident opinions through gentle questioning, remains the ancestor of both formal logic and modern seminar teaching; the Socratic method is still the working method of American law schools.

The death itself became one of the most heavily depicted scenes in western art. Jacques-Louis David's 1787 canvas The Death of Socrates, now in the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, distilled the Phaedo into a neoclassical composition in which the philosopher, one hand raised in argument, reaches for the cup while his disciples turn away in grief. The image, commissioned in the year the American constitutional convention met and painted two years before the fall of the Bastille, became a byword in the age of revolutions for the moral authority of the individual conscience against the state.

Beyond philosophy, the trial has been argued and re-argued in every generation as a test case for the tension between freedom of speech and democratic self-government. Modern commentators as different as I. F. Stone, in The Trial of Socrates (1988), and Robin Waterfield, in Why Socrates Died (2009), have offered opposing readings: was the trial a straightforward miscarriage by an insecure democracy scapegoating a dissident, or a comprehensible act of self-defence by a recently restored republic against a teacher whose closest associates had helped destroy it? The question is not settled and probably cannot be. It is, in every generation, a version of a question that Socrates himself might have asked.

image.caption: The Death of Socrates by Jacques-Louis David, 1787. Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York.
image.credit: Jacques-Louis David (1748 to 1825), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The Persian empire, restored to Egypt only briefly, had lost the Nile again in about 404 BCE when the local dynast Amyrtaeus expelled the Persian garrison and founded the short-lived Twenty-Eighth Dynasty. In Anatolia, the satraps of Sardis and Dascylium contended for influence in the Aegean, and the Spartan king Agesilaus was campaigning in Asia Minor. In Judea, the small Persian province of Yehud continued the rebuilding programme begun a century earlier under Ezra and Nehemiah, its temple community shaping what would become classical Judaism.

In China the Warring States period was already well underway following the traditional partition of Jin in 453 BCE. Seven great states, Qi, Chu, Yan, Han, Zhao, Wei and Qin, contended for supremacy while smaller polities were absorbed. Mozi, the founder of the Mohist school of universal love and defensive warfare, had died about 391 BCE; his followers would remain influential for another century. Iron farming tools were transforming agriculture; the earliest known Chinese cast-iron ploughs appear in this period.

On the Ganges plain the kingdom of Magadha, under the Nanda dynasty, was consolidating the dominance of the eastern Gangetic region that would in the next generation be inherited by the Mauryas. Buddhist and Jain monastic communities were expanding along the trade routes. Persian control of Gandhara and the Indus valley, established under Darius I, continued under his weakening successors; a young Macedonian would arrive at the Indus within seventy years.

In Mesoamerica the late Preclassic period was underway; the ceremonial city of Cuicuilco in the Basin of Mexico was at its peak, its circular pyramid a landmark, while in the Maya lowlands the earliest large stone architecture was being raised at El Mirador and Nakbé. In the Andes the Chavín cult centres were fading, replaced by regional cultures such as the Paracas on the south Peruvian coast, whose burial textiles are among the finest of the ancient world.

The Kushite kingdom of the middle Nile, based since around 590 BCE at Meroe, continued to build royal pyramids, temples and a distinctive Meroitic writing system. Along the Mediterranean coast Carthage, founded around 814 BCE by settlers from Phoenician Tyre, was expanding into Sicily and Sardinia, its wars with the Greek cities of Sicily under Dionysius I of Syracuse running through this decade. West and central African societies continued to develop iron-working traditions independently.

In the western Pacific the descendants of the Lapita voyagers had by now settled Tonga, Samoa and Fiji, consolidating ancestral Polynesian societies from which later expansions would reach the Society Islands and the Marquesas. In Australia, Aboriginal populations continued the world's oldest unbroken cultural tradition, developing rock-art traditions and complex kin systems across a continent of widely varying environments.

figures.name: Socrates
figures.role: Athenian philosopher, born c. 470 BCE, tried and executed by hemlock in 399 BCE on charges of impiety and corrupting the young
figures.credit: Roman marble bust after Lysippus, Louvre; photo Eric Gaba, CC BY-SA 2.5

figures.name: Plato
figures.role: Socrates's most influential pupil, whose dialogues Apology, Crito and Phaedo are the principal literary record of the trial and death
figures.credit: Roman copy after Silanion, Musei Capitolini; photo Marie-Lan Nguyen, CC BY 2.5

figures.name: Meletus
figures.role: Young Athenian tragedian who formally laid the charge of impiety against Socrates

figures.name: Anytus
figures.role: Influential democratic politician, the driving force behind the prosecution

figures.name: Xenophon
figures.role: Soldier and writer, another pupil, whose Memorabilia and Apology defend Socrates from a more prosaic angle than Plato
figures.credit: Roman bust, c. 120 CE; photograph Carole Raddato, CC BY-SA 2.0

images.caption: The Death of Socrates, oil on canvas by Jacques-Louis David, 1787. Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York. Socrates reaches for the cup of hemlock as Plato sits at the foot of the couch and Crito grasps his knee.
images.credit: Jacques-Louis David (1748 to 1825), via Wikimedia Commons
